threats.title: Token1 Premium Sign Inversion in NAV Calculation Systematically Misprices Shares
threats.hypothesis_description: In computeNAV, the token1 premium exposure is computed as `longPremium.leftSlot() - shortPremium.leftSlot()`, which is the OPPOSITE sign convention of token0's `shortPremium.rightSlot() - longPremium.rightSlot()`. For a vault that has net earned token1 premiums (short > long), the NAV subtracts earned premiums instead of adding them, systematically understating NAV. Conversely, owed premiums are added instead of subtracted, overstating NAV.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code-level sign error that directly corrupts the NAV used for all deposit and withdrawal fulfillment pricing. Any vault with Panoptic positions that accumulate token1 premiums will have incorrect share pricing, enabling value extraction during fulfillments.

proof_of_signal.pattern: poolExposure1 = int256(uint256(longPremium.leftSlot())) - int256(uint256(shortPremium.leftSlot())) uses inverted sign compared to poolExposure0 = int256(uint256(shortPremium.rightSlot())) - int256(uint256(longPremium.rightSlot()))

preconditions: Vault has open Panoptic positions that accumulate token1 premiums
shortPremium.leftSlot() differs from longPremium.leftSlot()
Manager calls fulfillDeposits or fulfillWithdrawals using the mispriced NAV

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: poolExposure1 = longPremium.leftSlot() - shortPremium.leftSlot() vs correct: shortPremium.leftSlot() - longPremium.leftSlot()
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Sign inversion of token1 premium contribution to NAV distorts share pricing automatically
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If net token1 premium earned = P, NAV is off by 2*P (swung from +P to -P). Depositors during understated NAV get excess shares; withdrawers during overstated NAV get excess assets.

economic_questions: What is the typical magnitude of token1 premium accumulation for active Panoptic strategies?
How does the 2x NAV swing affect share price for a typical vault TVL?
Can an external observer time deposit/withdrawal requests to exploit predictable NAV distortion?
validation_requirements: Verify Panoptic LeftRightUnsigned slot conventions for premium (leftSlot = token1, rightSlot = token0)
Compute NAV with correct and incorrect signs for realistic premium scenarios
Trace impact through fulfillDeposits/fulfillWithdrawals share pricing

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 2 * net_token1_premium value extracted per fulfillment cycle
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on premium magnitude relative to TVL

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When vault has large accumulated token1 premiums
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; bug exists in code logic

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All existing shareholders when NAV is understated; all withdrawers when NAV is overstated
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to their share of the vault relative to the premium magnitude
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All vault assets are at risk over time through cumulative mispricing

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical premium values for Panoptic options strategies
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Side-by-side NAV computation with correct vs inverted sign

threats.title: Per-Pool NAV Clamping to Zero Inflates NAV by Ignoring Underwater Pool Debt
threats.hypothesis_description: computeNAV sums NAV across pools using `nav += uint256(Math.max(poolExposure0 + poolExposure1, 0))`. Negative per-pool exposure (debt/loss) is clamped to zero instead of reducing the aggregate NAV. If Pool A has +100 exposure and Pool B has -80 exposure, NAV reports 100 instead of the correct 20. This systematically overstates NAV whenever any pool has negative net exposure.
threats.investigation_rationale: Overstated NAV directly corrupts fulfillment pricing. During deposit fulfillment, inflated totalAssets means depositors receive fewer shares than fair value. During withdrawal fulfillment, inflated totalAssets means withdrawers receive more assets than fair value, draining the vault. This occurs automatically in normal vault operations whenever pools have mixed profitability.

proof_of_signal.pattern: nav += uint256(Math.max(poolExposure0 + poolExposure1, 0)) clamps negative pool exposure to zero per-pool instead of aggregating then clamping

preconditions: Vault has positions in 2+ pools
At least one pool has negative net exposure (underwater)
At least one pool has positive net exposure

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: nav += uint256(Math.max(pool_i_exposure, 0)) for each pool; should be nav = max(sum(all_pool_exposures), 0)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Multi-pool vaults automatically overstate NAV when any pool is underwater
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: NAV inflation = sum of absolute values of negative pool exposures. E.g., if underwater pool has -50 exposure, NAV is overstated by 50.

economic_questions: How common is it for a multi-pool vault to have one underwater pool while others are profitable?
What is the maximum NAV inflation possible with typical Panoptic strategies?
Does the manager benefit from this by timing withdrawals during high NAV inflation?
validation_requirements: Model NAV calculation with multi-pool scenarios including one or more underwater pools
Compare per-pool clamped NAV vs aggregate clamped NAV for realistic portfolios
Verify downstream impact on fulfillDeposits and fulfillWithdrawals share pricing

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - occurs automatically during normal operations
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Equal to total absolute negative pool exposures across all underwater pools
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Proportional to underwater pool losses vs vault TVL
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Persistent while any pool is underwater

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During volatile markets when pools diverge in profitability
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market action needed; occurs from normal market movements

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Depositors (receive fewer shares) and remaining shareholders (diluted by overpaid withdrawers)
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to NAV inflation relative to vault TVL
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire vault TVL at risk during sustained multi-pool losses

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical number of pools and position exposure ranges
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Build test case with 2+ pools, one underwater, compare NAV outputs

threats.title: Double MulDiv Rounding in executeDeposit Systematically Shortchanges Depositors
threats.hypothesis_description: executeDeposit performs two consecutive mulDiv operations: first computing userAssetsDeposited = mulDiv(queuedAmount, assetsFulfilled, assetsDeposited), then sharesReceived = mulDiv(userAssetsDeposited, sharesReceived, assetsFulfilled). Each mulDiv rounds down independently, compounding the rounding error. A single-step mulDiv(queuedAmount, totalSharesReceived, assetsDeposited) would be more precise. Over many users and epochs, the orphaned dust shares accumulate in totalSupply but are permanently unclaimable.
threats.investigation_rationale: The double rounding is a code correctness issue that systematically disadvantages every depositor. While individual losses are small (dust), the aggregate over many users and epochs could become material, and the pattern represents a demonstrable code defect.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Two sequential Math.mulDiv calls with intermediate rounding: mulDiv(queued, fulfilled, deposited) then mulDiv(result, shares, fulfilled)

preconditions: Partial epoch fulfillment (assetsFulfilled < assetsDeposited)
Multiple users in the same epoch with non-trivial deposit amounts
Values not perfectly divisible

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Two sequential mulDiv with intermediate rounding vs single mulDiv(queuedAmount, sharesReceived, assetsDeposited)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Systematic per-user dust loss on every deposit execution
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Per-user loss is 0-1 wei per mulDiv, ~1-2 wei per execution. Over 1000 users × 100 epochs = up to 200K wei orphaned.

economic_questions: What is the maximum per-user dust loss per execution?
Does the loss compound across many epochs of partial fulfillment?
Can the orphaned shares in totalSupply affect share pricing over time?
validation_requirements: Compare double-mulDiv vs single-mulDiv outputs for typical deposit amounts
Calculate cumulative dust loss for realistic user counts and epoch counts
Verify that orphaned totalSupply doesn't materially affect share pricing

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Dust amounts per user; aggregate may be material over long periods

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Aggregate dust across all users and epochs

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical deposit amounts, number of users, partial fulfillment ratios
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Fuzz test comparing double vs single mulDiv precision loss

threats.title: Unset feeWallet (address(0)) Permanently Blocks All Profitable Withdrawal Executions
threats.hypothesis_description: feeWallet defaults to address(0) (never set in constructor). If performanceFeeBps > 0 and feeWallet is never set via setFeeWallet(), any executeWithdrawal where the user has profit (assetsToWithdraw > withdrawnBasis) will attempt SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer to address(0). Most ERC-20 tokens revert on transfer to address(0), permanently blocking all profitable withdrawal executions.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a configuration gap that creates a denial-of-service for all profitable withdrawals. Users who have already burned their shares via requestWithdrawal cannot recover them if the withdrawal execution reverts, effectively locking their funds until the owner sets a valid feeWallet.
deployment_assumptions: feeWallet is never set via setFeeWallet() after deployment
underlying token reverts on transfer to address(0)
threats.config_evidence: Constructor does not set feeWallet. No deployment script provided to verify post-deploy setup. feeWallet defaults to address(0) in storage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer(underlyingToken, feeWallet, performanceFee) where feeWallet can be address(0); no validation in constructor or executeWithdrawal

preconditions: performanceFeeBps > 0
feeWallet == address(0) (not set after deployment)
User's withdrawal has profit (assetsToWithdraw > withdrawnBasis)
Underlying token reverts on transfer to address(0)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No feeWallet validation in constructor or executeWithdrawal
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Configuration omission causes DoS on all profitable withdrawals
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit to attacker; victims lose access to withdrawal proceeds proportional to their burned shares

economic_questions: What is the total value of shares that could be burned before the issue is discovered?
Can the owner recover the situation by calling setFeeWallet after the fact?
Are there any ERC-20 tokens that allow transfer to address(0)?
validation_requirements: Verify feeWallet default value is address(0)
Test executeWithdrawal with feeWallet=address(0) and positive performanceFee
Confirm that setting feeWallet after the fact unblocks pending withdrawals

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - not an attack, configuration gap

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after deployment without proper setup

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with profitable withdrawal requests
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of their withdrawal is blocked (shares already burned)
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All profitable withdrawal proceeds in the vault

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify constructor does not set feeWallet; check deployment scripts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test revert path on executeWithdrawal with feeWallet=address(0)

threats.title: NAV Underflow in Fulfillment Functions Bricks Vault When Underwater
threats.hypothesis_description: Both fulfillDeposits and fulfillWithdrawals compute totalAssets as `computeNAV() + 1 - assetsDeposited - reservedWithdrawalAssets`. If the vault's NAV drops below assetsDeposited + reservedWithdrawalAssets (due to investment losses or market conditions), this subtraction underflows, reverting the transaction. The manager cannot fulfill any deposits or withdrawals, effectively bricking the vault. Users' deposited assets are locked with no withdrawal path since they haven't received shares.
threats.investigation_rationale: This underflow creates a dead-end state where the vault cannot process any epoch transitions. Users who deposited assets cannot get them back (cancelDeposit requires onlyManager, but even if called, the manager may not have funds to return if they were invested). The vault becomes permanently stuck until NAV recovers above the threshold.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalAssets = accountant.computeNAV(...) + 1 - epochState.assetsDeposited - reservedWithdrawalAssets; unchecked subtraction underflows if NAV < deposits + reserved

preconditions: Vault has significant pending deposits (assetsDeposited) or reserved withdrawal assets
Manager invests deposited funds and incurs losses, or market conditions drop NAV
computeNAV() + 1 < assetsDeposited + reservedWithdrawalAssets

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Unsigned subtraction: NAV + 1 - deposits - reserved can underflow
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Market losses or ECON-H001/H002 NAV miscalculations trigger underflow
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; DoS locks all user funds in the vault

economic_questions: What percentage NAV drop triggers the underflow for typical vault configurations?
Can cancelDeposit/cancelWithdrawal still function during this state?
Does ECON-H002 (per-pool clamping) or ECON-H001 (sign inversion) make this underflow more likely?
validation_requirements: Compute threshold NAV that triggers underflow for various deposit/reserve levels
Test recovery path: can manager cancel deposits to reduce assetsDeposited?
Verify interaction with NAV clamping bugs

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until NAV recovers or manager cancels all deposits

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Large pending deposits increase vulnerability threshold

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with pending deposits or withdrawals
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All pending deposits plus all reserved withdrawal assets

threats.title: Zero Fulfillment Amount Creates Permanently Stuck User Funds via Division-by-Zero Epoch
threats.hypothesis_description: If the manager calls fulfillDeposits(0, ...) or fulfillWithdrawals(0, ...), the epoch state records 0 for assetsFulfilled/sharesFulfilled. The epoch advances, and the aggregate unfulfilled amount rolls over correctly. However, individual users' executeDeposit/executeWithdrawal for that epoch will revert with division by zero (mulDiv denominator = 0). Since the function reverts, the per-user rollover to epoch+1 never occurs. The user's deposit/withdrawal is permanently stuck in the zero-fulfilled epoch with no recovery path.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a missing input validation that creates an irrecoverable fund-locking state. The aggregate epoch rollover diverges from per-user state, making individual user funds inaccessible. cancelDeposit/cancelWithdrawal only work for the current epoch, not past epochs, so the manager cannot help after the epoch advances further.

proof_of_signal.pattern: executeDeposit: Math.mulDiv(userAssetsDeposited, sharesReceived, assetsFulfilled) where assetsFulfilled=0 causes division by zero; no validation in fulfillDeposits that assetsToFulfill > 0

preconditions: Manager calls fulfillDeposits(0, ...) or fulfillWithdrawals(0, ...)
Users have deposits/withdrawals queued in the affected epoch
Epoch advances beyond the zero-fulfilled epoch (making cancelDeposit/cancelWithdrawal inaccessible)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No require(assetsToFulfill > 0) or require(sharesToFulfill > 0) in fulfill functions
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Manager error or malice creates irrecoverable epoch state
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit extraction; user funds permanently locked in zero-fulfilled epoch

economic_questions: Is there any recovery mechanism for funds stuck in a zero-fulfilled epoch?
Can the manager use manage() to manually recover stuck funds?
How quickly does the epoch advance past the stuck epoch?
validation_requirements: Test fulfillDeposits(0, ...) and verify executeDeposit reverts for affected users
Verify cancelDeposit cannot access past epochs
Test whether manage() can manually transfer stuck deposits back to users

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - manager error, not capital-based attack

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with deposits/withdrawals in the zero-fulfilled epoch
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of queued deposit amount or burned shares permanently lost
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all queued deposits or withdrawal entitlements in the affected epoch

threats.title: Oracle Time-Based Staleness Not Validated Allowing Outdated Prices in NAV
threats.hypothesis_description: computeNAV validates manager-provided prices against TWAP oracles using a deviation band check, but never verifies the TWAP oracle's last update timestamp. If the underlying Uniswap pool has low activity, the TWAP uses stale observations that may be hours or days old. The manager can provide prices within maxPriceDeviation of the stale TWAP, but far from actual market prices, leading to incorrect NAV calculation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deviation check only ensures consistency between manager price and TWAP, not that either reflects current market conditions. A stale TWAP becomes the reference point, and the manager (even acting honestly) may not realize the TWAP is outdated, especially for less liquid pools.
deployment_assumptions: Pool oracle (poolOracle, oracle0, oracle1) uses a low-activity Uniswap pool
twapWindow is set but pool has no observations within that window
threats.config_evidence: PanopticVaultAccountant.sol: PoolInfo struct has twapWindow parameter but no timestamp freshness field. PanopticMath.twapFilter is called without any timestamp validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Math.abs(managerPrice - PanopticMath.twapFilter(oracle, twapWindow)) > maxPriceDeviation checks deviation but not underlying oracle freshness timestamp

preconditions: One or more oracles used in computeNAV have stale observations
Actual market price has diverged significantly from stale TWAP
Manager provides price within deviation band of stale TWAP

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: TWAP deviation check without timestamp freshness validation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Stale oracle allows NAV to be calculated at outdated prices
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: NAV mispricing proportional to (actual_price - stale_price) * position_size

economic_questions: What is the realistic staleness window for typical Uniswap pools used as oracles?
How much can prices diverge over the maximum staleness period?
Does the twapWindow parameter mitigate staleness for actively traded pools?
validation_requirements: Analyze Uniswap observation frequency for target pools
Calculate NAV error for realistic staleness scenarios
Test PanopticMath.twapFilter behavior with stale observations

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Proportional to vault TVL for profit extraction
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to price divergence × position size
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on pool liquidity and observation frequency
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single block fulfillment with stale prices

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During high volatility with low-activity oracle pools
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market action needed; relies on natural staleness

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Depositors or withdrawers depending on direction of price staleness
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to their share of the mispriced epoch
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Fulfilled deposit/withdrawal amounts in the affected epoch

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical twapWindow values, target pool observation frequencies
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test computeNAV with simulated stale oracle data

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token as Underlying Creates Accounting Mismatch and Last-Withdrawer Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: requestDeposit records the full `assets` parameter into queuedDeposit and depositEpochState.assetsDeposited, but SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom transfers fewer tokens to the vault if the underlying is a fee-on-transfer token. The vault's actual balance is less than the recorded deposits, creating a growing shortfall. Early withdrawers receive full entitlements, while later withdrawers face insufficient balance and reverted transfers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract performs no received-amount validation after the transfer. If deployed with a fee-on-transfer underlying token, the accounting mismatch grows with every deposit, eventually causing insolvency. The documentation states 'Valid EIP-20 tokens' which technically includes fee-on-transfer tokens.
deployment_assumptions: underlyingToken is a fee-on-transfer ERC-20 token
threats.config_evidence: HypoVault.sol constructor accepts any address as underlyingToken. No balance-before/after check in requestDeposit. Project docs say 'Valid EIP-20 tokens' but fee-on-transfer is EIP-20 compliant.

proof_of_signal.pattern: queuedDeposit += assets; safeTransferFrom(assets) without checking actual received amount; no balance delta validation

preconditions: underlyingToken is a fee-on-transfer ERC-20 token
Multiple users deposit over time, accumulating shortfall
Users execute withdrawals, draining actual balance faster than recorded

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: queuedDeposit[user][epoch] += assets records nominal amount; vault receives assets - fee
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Growing accounting shortfall between recorded deposits and actual balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early withdrawers extract full value; last withdrawers face insolvency

economic_questions: What fee-on-transfer tokens are likely deployment candidates?
How quickly does the shortfall grow relative to deposit volume?
Can an attacker deliberately exploit the shortfall by timing withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Test requestDeposit with a mock fee-on-transfer token
Calculate shortfall accumulation rate for typical fee percentages
Verify executeWithdrawal behavior when vault balance < reservedWithdrawalAssets

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to total accumulated fee shortfall extractable by early withdrawers
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Equal to the transfer fee percentage accumulated across all deposits

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Early withdrawal before shortfall causes reverts

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Last withdrawers unable to execute withdrawals
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative transfer fees across all deposits

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Candidate underlying tokens and their fee structures
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model shortfall growth for common fee-on-transfer rates

threats.title: Manager Can Drain reservedWithdrawalAssets via manage() Breaking Withdrawal Solvency
threats.hypothesis_description: The manage() function allows the manager to execute arbitrary calls from the vault, including ERC-20 transfers of the underlying token. There is no check that the vault retains at least reservedWithdrawalAssets worth of underlying tokens after the call. A compromised or malicious manager can drain all reserved assets, making fulfilled but unexecuted withdrawals permanently insolvent.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the manager is trusted per the threat model, this represents a critical solvency invariant violation. Users who burned their shares via requestWithdrawal and had their withdrawal fulfilled are entitled to their pro-rata assets. The manage() function can silently break this entitlement with no safeguard, time delay, or on-chain alert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: manage() executes arbitrary calls with no post-condition check that IERC20(underlyingToken).balanceOf(vault) >= reservedWithdrawalAssets

preconditions: Manager is compromised or acts maliciously
reservedWithdrawalAssets > 0 (fulfilled but unexecuted withdrawals exist)
Manager calls manage() to transfer underlying tokens to an external address

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No post-call balance check in manage()
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: manage(underlyingToken, transfer(attacker, amount), 0) drains reserved assets
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker extracts up to reservedWithdrawalAssets worth of underlying tokens

economic_questions: What is the typical reservedWithdrawalAssets balance?
Could a minimum balance check be added to manage() without breaking legitimate operations?
Are there monitoring tools that would detect this drain?
validation_requirements: Test manage() call that transfers underlying tokens out
Verify executeWithdrawal reverts after reserve drain
Evaluate feasibility of post-call balance invariant check

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After large withdrawal fulfillment with high reservedWithdrawalAssets
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Direct token theft, no market impact

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with fulfilled but unexecuted withdrawals

threats.title: Manager NAV Bias via maxPriceDeviation Band Enables Systematic Value Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: The manager provides tick prices to computeNAV that are validated to be within maxPriceDeviation of the TWAP. The manager can consistently pick prices at the extreme of the allowed band to systematically inflate NAV during deposit fulfillment (diluting new depositors) or deflate NAV during withdrawal fulfillment (underpaying withdrawers). This creates a persistent extraction opportunity bounded by the deviation band.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even a small per-epoch bias compounds over many fulfillment cycles. The deviation band is a security parameter, but its width directly determines the manager's ability to bias pricing. A wide deviation band (needed for volatile assets) also creates a larger extraction window.
deployment_assumptions: maxPriceDeviation is set to a non-trivial value (e.g., > 100 ticks)
threats.config_evidence: PanopticVaultAccountant.sol: PoolInfo.maxPriceDeviation is a configurable parameter set via updatePoolsHash. No deployment script to verify actual value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Math.abs(managerPrice - TWAP) <= maxPriceDeviation allows manager to choose prices at band extremes for each pool and conversion oracle

preconditions: Manager acts maliciously or collusively
maxPriceDeviation is wide enough to materially affect NAV
Vault has significant positions requiring tick-based valuation

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Manager chooses poolPrice, token0Price, token1Price within deviation band
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Systematic band-edge pricing across all 3 price inputs per pool
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Per-epoch extraction up to deviation_band_width × position_notional × number_of_price_inputs

economic_questions: What are typical maxPriceDeviation values in production?
How does compounding across multiple pools amplify the bias?
What is the annualized extraction rate for realistic parameters?
validation_requirements: Calculate NAV at band extremes vs center for typical position portfolios
Model cumulative extraction over 100+ fulfillment cycles
Compare extraction magnitude to typical vault returns

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 0.1-1% per fulfillment cycle depending on deviation band width

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Depositors or withdrawers depending on bias direction
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to deviation band width and position sizes
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative extraction across all fulfillment cycles

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Realistic maxPriceDeviation values and position sizes
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: NAV sensitivity analysis to price deviations

threats.title: No User Self-Cancel Mechanism Creates Permanent Fund Lock If Manager Becomes Unavailable
threats.hypothesis_description: cancelDeposit and cancelWithdrawal are both onlyManager. If the manager becomes unresponsive (key loss, compromise, dispute), users have no mechanism to recover queued assets. The owner can change the manager via setManager, but even a new manager must actively process cancellations. If both owner and manager are unavailable, user funds are permanently locked with no timeout-based escape hatch.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the trust model states managers are trusted, the absence of a user self-cancel mechanism creates a systemic risk where a single point of failure (manager availability) can lock arbitrary amounts of user funds. This is a protocol design gap that becomes critical if the manager key is lost or if there's a governance dispute.

proof_of_signal.pattern: cancelDeposit is onlyManager with no user-callable alternative or timeout-based self-cancel

preconditions: Manager becomes unavailable (key loss, compromise, dispute)
Owner is also unavailable or unable to set a new responsive manager
Users have pending deposit or withdrawal requests

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No timeout-based self-cancel: users must rely entirely on manager availability
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Manager unavailability locks all pending user funds indefinitely
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit extraction; pure fund-locking DoS for all queued users

economic_questions: What is the expected manager uptime and key management setup?
Is there a governance process to replace an unavailable manager?
What is the maximum TVL that could be locked in pending requests?
validation_requirements: Verify no alternative path exists for users to recover queued assets
Assess whether owner can fully recover the situation via setManager
Evaluate whether a time-delayed self-cancel mechanism is feasible

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with pending deposits or withdrawals
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of queued deposit or burned withdrawal shares locked
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total pending deposits + value of shares in pending withdrawals

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Manager key management setup and governance procedures
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify all code paths require manager for fund recovery
